statement.p: Consider the set of symmetric n\times n-matrices, i.e., matrices satisfying A=A^T. Show that this set of symmetric matrices is a subspace of \Mvec_{n,n}, the vector space of all n\times n-matrices.
Consider the set of all vectors \begin{bmatrix}x\\y\end{bmatrix}\in\mathbb{R}^2 such that x+y\ge 0. Is this a subspace of \mathbb{R}^2?

solution.p: No. It is not closed under scalar multiplication.

statement.p: Consider the set of all vectors \begin{bmatrix}x\\y\end{bmatrix}\in\mathbb{R}^2 such that xy=0. Is this a subspace of \mathbb{R}^2?

solution.p: No. It is not closed under addition.

statement.p: Let V be the set of those polynomials ax^2+bx+c\in \mathbb{P}_2 such that a+b+c=0. Is V a subspace of \mathbb{P}_2? Explain.

Let U,W be subspaces of a vector space V and consider U+W defined as the set of all vectors that can be written of the form \uvec+\wvec, where \uvec\in U and \wvec\in W. Show that U+W is a subspace of V.
Let U,W be subspaces of a vector space V. Then U\cap W consists of all vectors which are in both U and W. Show that U\cap W is a subspace of V.

Let U,W be subspaces of a vector space V. Then U\cup W consists of all vectors which are in either U or W. Show that U\cup W is not necessarily a subspace of V by giving an example where U\cup W fails to be a subspace.

Let
U=\left\{ \begin{bmatrix}x\\y\\z\end{bmatrix}\in\mathbb{R}^3 \ \middle|\ |x|\le 4 \right\}.
Is U a subspace of \mathbb{R}^3?

Let
U=\left\{ A\in \Mvec_{2,2}(\mathbb{R}) \ \middle|\ A\begin{bmatrix}1\amp 0\\1\amp -1\end{bmatrix} = \begin{bmatrix}1\amp 1\\0\amp -1\end{bmatrix}A \right\}.
Show that U is a subspace of \Mvec_{2,2}(\mathbb{R}).

Let W be the subset of \mathbb{P}_3 given by
W=\{ax^3+bx^2+cx+d \mid a,b,c,d\in \mathbb{R},\ d=0\}.
Is W a subspace of \mathbb{P}_3?

Let W be the subset of \mathbb{P}_3 given by
W=\{p(x) \mid p(2)=1\}.
Is W a subspace of \mathbb{P}_3?

Let W be the subset of \mathrm{Seq}_{\mathbb{R}} consisting of all real-valued sequences that are alternating, i.e., where a_i\ge 0 for even i and a_i\le 0 for odd i, or vice versa. Is W a subspace of \mathrm{Seq}_{\mathbb{R}}?

Let W be the subset of \mathrm{Seq}_{\mathbb{R}} consisting of all real-valued sequences that satisfy the recurrence relation
a_{n+3}=a_{n+2}+2a_{n+1}-a_n.
Is W a subspace of \mathrm{Seq}_{\mathbb{R}}?

A sequence (a_i)_{i\in\mathbb{N}} is called periodic if there exists some k>0 such that for all i, a_{i+k}=a_i. The number k is called a period of the sequence. For example, the following is a periodic sequence with period 3:
(1,5,-7,1,5,-7,1,5,-7,1,5,-7,1,\ldots).

ol.li: Show that the set of all periodic sequences of a fixed period k forms a subspace of \mathrm{Seq}_{\mathbb{R}}.
More difficult: show that the set of all periodic sequences of all periods forms a subspace of \mathrm{Seq}_{\mathbb{R}}.

statement.p: A function f:\mathbb{R}\to\mathbb{R} is called symmetric if f(x)=f(-x) for all x\in\mathbb{R}. Let W be the subset of \mathrm{Func}_{\mathbb{R},\mathbb{R}} consisting of all symmetric functions. Is W a subspace of \mathrm{Func}_{\mathbb{R},\mathbb{R}}?

A function f:\mathbb{R}\to\mathbb{R} is said to vanish at infinity if \lim_{x\to\infty}f(x)=0 and \lim_{x\to-\infty}f(x)=0. Let W be the subset of \mathrm{Func}_{\mathbb{R},\mathbb{R}} consisting of all functions that vanish at infinity. Prove that W is a subspace of \mathrm{Func}_{\mathbb{R},\mathbb{R}}.
